Title: Selection Sort
Description: A sorting algorithm that repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion and places it at the beginning.
Explanation:
Selection Sort divides the array into two parts: a sorted portion on the left and an unsorted portion on the right. In each pass, it selects the smallest element from the unsorted portion and swaps it with the first element of that unsorted portion.

**How it works step by step:**
- Assume the first element of the unsorted portion is the minimum.
- Scan the rest of the unsorted portion to find the actual minimum.
- Swap the found minimum with the first element of the unsorted portion.
- Move the boundary between sorted and unsorted one step to the right.
- Repeat until the entire array is sorted.

**Example:** `[64, 25, 12, 22, 11]`
- Pass 1: min=11 (index 4). Swap with index 0. → `[11, 25, 12, 22, 64]`
- Pass 2: min=12 (index 2). Swap with index 1. → `[11, 12, 25, 22, 64]`
- Pass 3: min=22 (index 3). Swap with index 2. → `[11, 12, 22, 25, 64]`
- Pass 4: min=25 (index 3). Already in place. → `[11, 12, 22, 25, 64]`

**Key property:** Selection Sort always makes exactly **n-1 swaps** regardless of input — making it useful when write operations are expensive (e.g. flash memory). However it never benefits from a partially sorted input — it always runs O(n²) comparisons.
